blocks.text: He gave them a fixed number of days, but granted them authority over everything on the earth.
He endowed them with strength like his own, and made them in his own image.
He put the fear of them in all living beings, and gave them dominion over beasts and birds.
Discretion and tongue and eyes, ears and a mind for thinking he gave them.
He filled them with knowledge and understanding, and showed them good and evil.
16.26–17.7 : An interpretation of Gen 1–3.
6–7 : , in Genesis these things come to humans as a result of disobedience (Gen 2.4–3.24). Ben Sira ignores that part of the story.
He put the fear of him into their hearts to show them the majesty of his works.
to proclaim the grandeur of his works.
And they will praise his holy name,
He bestowed knowledge upon them, and allotted to them the law of life.
11 : , the Torah; see 45.5; Deut 30.15–20.
He established with them an eternal covenant, and revealed to them his decrees.
Their eyes saw his glorious majesty, and their ears heard the glory of his voice.
12–13 : , a reference to the Sinai theophany and (Ex 19).
He said to them, “Beware of all evil.” And he gave commandment to each of them concerning the neighbor.
11–14 : A shift to Israel and the giving of the Torah.
Their ways are always known to him; they will not be hid from his eyes.
He appointed a ruler for every nation, but Israel is the Lord's own portion.
All their works are as clear as the sun before him, and his eyes are ever upon their ways.
Their iniquities are not hidden from him, and all their sins are before the Lord.
One's almsgiving is like a signet ring with the Lord, and he will keep a person's kindness like the apple of his eye.
22 : (Jer 22.24; Hag 2.23) and (Deut 32.10) suggest a special relationship with God.
Afterward he will rise up and repay them, and he will bring their recompense on their heads.
Yet to those who repent he grants a return, and he encourages those who are losing hope.
16.24–17.24: Creation and repentance. A meditation on creation that moves from creation to the giving of the law to Israel, and individuals and repentance.
15–24 : God sees human sin (cf. 15.19) and judges, but and repentance allow .
Turn back to the Lord and forsake your sins; pray in his presence and lessen your offense.
Return to the Most High and turn away from iniquity, and hate intensely what he abhors.
Who will sing praises to the Most High in Hades in place of the living who give thanks?
From the dead, as from one who does not exist, thanksgiving has ceased; those who are alive and well sing the Lord's praises.
27–28 : The dead in (see 14.12n) cannot praise God (cf., e.g., Pss 6.5; 30.9; 88.10–12; Bar 2.17), which should be humanity's response to God (39.12–35; 43.11,27–33).
How great is the mercy of the Lord, and his forgiveness for those who return to him!
For not everything is within human capability, since human beings are not immortal.
What is brighter than the sun? Yet it can be eclipsed. So flesh and blood devise evil.
30–31 : If the sun's light can fail, how much more can .
He marshals the host of the height of heaven; but all human beings are dust and ashes.
17.25–32: Mercy and repentance. God's mercy allows repentance.
